name: Itzpapalotl
description: Itzpapalotl, meaning "Obsidian Butterfly" or "Clawed Butterfly," is a fearsome skeletal goddess in Aztec mythology. She is a powerful figure associated with agriculture, particularly the maize harvest, and is considered one of the Tzitzimime, monstrous star demons that threatened to devour humanity during solar eclipses or at the end of calendrical cycles. Itzpapalotl's origins are complex, potentially pre-dating the Aztecs themselves. She is often depicted with butterfly wings adorned with obsidian blades or flint knives, symbolizing her dual nature of beauty and terror, creation and destruction. Her skeletal face and clawed hands further emphasize her connection to death and the underworld. As a Tzitzimitl, she played a crucial role in the cosmic struggle to maintain the balance of the universe, preventing the collapse of the sky and the annihilation of humankind. Itzpapalotl is also associated with the paradise of Tamoanchan, the mythical place of origin for humanity, where she is sometimes depicted as a nurturing figure. She is also connected to warfare and sacrifice, representing the dangerous power of female warriors and the necessary bloodshed for cosmic renewal. Her influence extends to the arts, magic, and the protection of specific lineages, making her a multifaceted and enigmatic deity within the Aztec pantheon. She is a reminder of the inherent duality of life and death, beauty and horror, creation and destruction that permeates the Aztec worldview. Itzpapalotl's power lay in her ability to both nurture and destroy, reflecting the cyclical nature of existence and the constant struggle for survival.
subtitle: Obsidian Butterfly: Goddess of Terror

keyMyths.title: Itzpapalotl and the Tzitzimime
keyMyths.description: Itzpapalotl leads the Tzitzimime in their cosmic battle to prevent the end of the world. They descend during solar eclipses to devour humans.

keyMyths.title: Itzpapalotl in Tamoanchan
keyMyths.description: Itzpapalotl is sometimes associated with Tamoanchan, the paradise where humans originated. In this role, she is seen as a nurturing figure.

keyMyths.title: Itzpapalotl and Warfare
keyMyths.description: Itzpapalotl's association with warfare highlights the power and ferocity of female warriors. She represents the bloodshed necessary for cosmic renewal.
keyMyths.source: Durán, Diego. The History of the Indies of New Spain
